architecture.summary: This is a survey and taxonomy paper that analyzes structure-enhanced LLM reasoning schemes. It formalizes the prompt execution pipeline (preprocessing, context management, LLM generation, post-processing) and introduces a blueprint for reasoning topologies—graphs of thoughts where nodes represent semantic units of task resolution and edges represent dependencies. The paper does not propose a new trainable system, but rather provides a conceptual framework for understanding how existing schemes (Chain-of-Thought, Tree of Thoughts, Graph of Thoughts) map reasoning processes onto prompt/reply sequences.

biblio.title: Demystifying Chains, Trees, and Graphs of Thoughts

claims.statement: Prompt engineering coupled with structures has emerged as a promising paradigm, with designs such as Chain-of-Thought, Tree of Thoughts, or Graph of Thoughts, in which the overall LLM reasoning is guided by a structure such as a graph, significantly enhancing the LLM's capability to solve numerous tasks, ranging from logical or mathematical reasoning to planning or creative writing.

The prompt execution pipeline can be formally decomposed into five functional building blocks: preprocessing transformation P, context update transformation Φ, LLM auto-generative execution, post-processing transformation Q, and context update transformation Ψ, forming equations that generalize all prompting interactions.

A reasoning topology is defined as a graph G=(V,E) where V is a set of nodes modeling thoughts (semantic units of task resolution) and E is a set of edges modeling reasoning dependencies between thoughts.

Seven fundamental aspects define any LLM reasoning scheme that harnesses reasoning topologies: topology class (chain, tree, or graph), topology scope (single-prompt vs. multi-prompt), topology representation (implicit vs. explicit), topology derivation (user-constructed, automatic, or semi-automatic), reasoning schedule (how the topology is traversed), schedule representation (natural language, code, in-context examples, or other), and harnessed parts of the AI pipeline (pre-training, fine-tuning, retrieval, tools, modalities).

Two fundamental use cases of thoughts and topologies are distinguished: solution topologies (nodes reachable from the input task statement node that form steps toward solving the task) and in-context example topologies (nodes not reachable from the input, providing examples but not reasoning steps toward the solution).

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) introduces chain topologies with intermediate reasoning steps; Zero-shot-CoT elicits multi-step reasoning without hand-tuned in-context examples; Least-to-Most Prompting decomposes complex tasks into sub-questions solved sequentially; Tree of Thoughts (ToT) allows branching at any point with state evaluation and search algorithms (BFS/DFS); Graph of Thoughts (GoT) enables arbitrary reasoning dependencies including both branching and aggregation operations.

CoT significantly outperforms Input-Output (IO) prompting in arithmetic reasoning on benchmarks such as GSM8K (Grade School Math), SVAMP (Simple Variations on Arithmetic Math word Problem), and MAWPS (MAth Word Problem).

State-of-the-art prompting schemes still exhibit numerous limitations: they are limited to simple tasks such as Game of 24; state-of-the-art prompting schemes often entail high inference costs; designing, developing, maintaining, and extending these schemes is hard due to rapid LLM ecosystem development and unclear definitions of concepts such as how 'thought' relates to 'prompt'.
